questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels with a constant velocity parallel to the $X$-axis. Its angular momentum relative to the origin
questions.solution:
Let the particle move along a line parallel to the $X$-axis with a constant velocity $\vec{v} = v \hat{i}$.

The position vector of the particle at any time $t$ is $\vec{r} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$.

Since the particle moves parallel to the $X$-axis, its $y$ and $z$ coordinates remain constant.

The angular momentum of the particle relative to the origin is given by $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$, where $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ is the linear momentum.

$\vec{L} = (x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}) \times (mv \hat{i})$

$\vec{L} = m v (x (\hat{i} \times \hat{i}) + y (\hat{j} \times \hat{i}) + z (\hat{k} \times \hat{i}))$

$\vec{L} = m v (0 - y \hat{k} + z \hat{j})$

$\vec{L} = m z v \hat{j} - m y v \hat{k}$

Since $m$, $v$, $y$, and $z$ are all constants, the angular momentum $\vec{L}$ remains constant in both magnitude and direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $\vec{A}$ represents a unit vector directed along the axis of rotation of a purely rotating body, and $\vec{B}$ is a unit vector directed along the velocity of a particle $P$ of the body situated away from the axis. The value of $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ is
questions.solution:
For a purely rotating body, the velocity $\vec{v}$ of any particle $P$ situated away from the axis of rotation is given by $\vec{v} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$, where $\vec{\omega}$ is the angular velocity vector and $\vec{r}$ is the position vector of the particle.

The angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}$ is directed along the axis of rotation.

From the properties of the cross product, the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{r}$.

Since $\vec{A}$ is a unit vector along the axis of rotation (parallel to $\vec{\omega}$) and $\vec{B}$ is a unit vector along the velocity $\vec{v}$, $\vec{A}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{B}$.

$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}| |\vec{B}| \cos(90^{\circ}) = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body executes pure rotation. The angular velocity $\omega$ of the body, the distance $r$ of a particle from the axis, and the linear speed $v$ of the particle satisfy the relation $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$. Therefore,
questions.solution:
For a rigid body executing pure rotation, all particles of the body rotate with the same angular velocity $\omega$.

The linear speed $v$ of a particle at a distance $r$ from the axis of rotation is given by $v = \omega r$.

Since $\omega$ is the same for all particles of the rigid body, it does not depend on the distance $r$ of the particle from the axis of rotation.

Therefore, $\omega$ is independent of $r$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body rotates uniformly about a fixed vertical axis in an inertial frame. The resultant force acting on a particle of the body not situated on the axis is
options: horizontal and skew with the axis
questions.solution:
Let the fixed vertical axis of rotation be the z-axis. The angular velocity of the body is $\vec{\omega} = \omega \hat{k}$.

Since the body rotates uniformly, the angular acceleration $\vec{\alpha} = 0$.

The position vector of a particle not on the axis can be written as $\vec{r} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$.

The acceleration of the particle is given by the centripetal acceleration, as the tangential acceleration is zero:

$\vec{a} = \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$

$\vec{a} = \omega \hat{k} \times (\omega \hat{k} \times (x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k})) = \omega \hat{k} \times (\omega x \hat{j} - \omega y \hat{i}) = -\omega^2 x \hat{i} - \omega^2 y \hat{j}$

$\vec{a} = -\omega^2 (x \hat{i} + y \hat{j})$

According to Newton's second law, the resultant force on the particle is $\vec{F} = m\vec{a} = -m\omega^2 (x \hat{i} + y \hat{j})$.

This force has no z-component, meaning it is entirely horizontal. Furthermore, it is directed towards the origin of the xy-plane, which means its line of action intersects the axis of rotation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body rotates uniformly about an axis fixed in an inertial frame of reference. Let $\vec{A}$ be a unit vector along the rotation axis and $\vec{B}$ be a unit vector along the resultant force acting on a particle $P$ of the body located away from the axis. The value of $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ is
questions.solution:
Since the body rotates uniformly about a fixed axis, its angular acceleration is zero.

The particle $P$ moves in a circular path centered on the axis of rotation. The only acceleration of the particle is the centripetal acceleration, which is directed towards the center of its circular path.

This means the resultant force $\vec{F}$ acting on the particle is directed towards the axis and is perpendicular to the axis of rotation.

Since $\vec{A}$ is along the axis of rotation and $\vec{B}$ is along the resultant force, the angle between them is $90^{\circ}$.

Therefore, $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos 90^{\circ} = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a force $\vec{F}$ that acts on a particle with position vector $\vec{r}$. If $\vec{\Gamma}$ represents the torque of this force about the origin, which of the following is true?
questions.solution:
Torque is defined as the cross product of the position vector and the force vector:

$\vec{\Gamma} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$

By the properties of the cross product, the resulting vector $\vec{\Gamma}$ is always perpendicular to both $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$.

Since the dot product of two perpendicular vectors is zero, we have:

$\vec{r} \cdot \vec{\Gamma} = \vec{r} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F}) = 0$

$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{\Gamma} = \vec{F} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F}) = 0$

Thus, both dot products are zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ has one end clamped. It rests on a smooth horizontal surface, rotating about the clamped end with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$. The horizontal component of the force applied by the clamp on the rod is
questions.solution:
The center of mass of the uniform rod is at a distance $r_{cm} = \dfrac{l}{2}$ from the clamped end.

The rod is rotating with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$ on a smooth horizontal surface.

The centripetal acceleration of the center of mass is $a_{cm} = \omega^2 r_{cm} = \omega^2 \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)$.

The net horizontal force on the rod is provided by the clamp.

Using Newton's second law, the horizontal component of the force applied by the clamp is $F = m a_{cm} = m \omega^2 \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 l$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rod is kept vertically at a point $O$ on a smooth horizontal surface. After being slightly rotated and released, it falls onto the horizontal surface. The lower end will be located
options: at a distance less than $l/2$ from $O$
at a distance larger than $l/2$ from $O$
questions.solution:
Since the horizontal surface is smooth, there is no horizontal force acting on the rod.

The initial horizontal velocity of the center of mass is zero. Therefore, the horizontal position of the center of mass remains unchanged during the fall.

Initially, the rod is placed vertically at point $O$, so its center of mass lies exactly above $O$.

When the rod falls and lies completely on the horizontal surface, its center of mass will be at point $O$.

Since the rod is uniform, its center of mass is located at its midpoint, which is at a distance $l/2$ from either end.

Thus, the lower end of the rod will be at a distance $l/2$ from $O$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular disc $A$ of radius $r$ is fabricated from an iron plate of thickness $t$, and a second circular disc $B$ of radius $4r$ is fabricated from an iron plate of thickness $t/4$. The relationship between the moments of inertia $I_A$ and $I_B$ is
options: depends on the actual values of $t$ and $r$
questions.solution:
Let the density of iron be $\rho$.

The mass of disc A is $M_A = \pi r^2 t \rho$

The moment of inertia of disc A about its central axis is $I_A = \dfrac{1}{2} M_A r^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (\pi r^2 t \rho) r^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi \rho t r^4$

The mass of disc B is $M_B = \pi (4r)^2 \left(\dfrac{t}{4}\right) \rho = 4 \pi r^2 t \rho$

The moment of inertia of disc B about its central axis is $I_B = \dfrac{1}{2} M_B (4r)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (4 \pi r^2 t \rho) (16r^2) = 32 \pi \rho t r^4$

Comparing $I_A$ and $I_B$, we get $I_B = 64 I_A$

Therefore, $I_A < I_B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two circular discs $A$ and $B$ are made of the same material. Disc $A$ has radius $r$ and thickness $t$, while disc $B$ has radius $4r$ and thickness $t/4$. Equal torques act on both discs, initially at rest. At a later instant, the linear speeds of a point on the rim of $A$ and a point on the rim of $B$ are $v_A$ and $v_B$ respectively. We have
options: the relation depends on the actual magnitude of the torques
questions.solution:
Mass of disc $A$ is $m_A = \rho \pi r^2 t$

Mass of disc $B$ is $m_B = \rho \pi (4r)^2 \left(\dfrac{t}{4}\right) = 4 \rho \pi r^2 t = 4m_A$

Moment of inertia of disc $A$ is $I_A = \dfrac{1}{2} m_A r^2$

Moment of inertia of disc $B$ is $I_B = \dfrac{1}{2} m_B (4r)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (4m_A) (16r^2) = 64 I_A$

Since equal torques $\tau$ act on both discs, their angular accelerations are $\alpha_A = \dfrac{\tau}{I_A}$ and $\alpha_B = \dfrac{\tau}{I_B} = \dfrac{\tau}{64 I_A} = \dfrac{\alpha_A}{64}$

At a later instant $t_0$, the angular speeds are $\omega_A = \alpha_A t_0$ and $\omega_B = \alpha_B t_0 = \dfrac{\omega_A}{64}$

The linear speeds of points on the rims are $v_A = \omega_A r$ and $v_B = \omega_B (4r) = \left(\dfrac{\omega_A}{64}\right) (4r) = \dfrac{\omega_A r}{16} = \dfrac{v_A}{16}$

Therefore, $v_A > v_B$

Answer: $v_A > v_B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, a small wheel is fixed coaxially onto a larger wheel of double the radius. The entire system rotates about their common axis. The strings supporting $A$ and $B$ do not slip over the wheels. If $x$ and $y$ represent the distances travelled by $A$ and $B$ during the same time interval, then
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the smaller wheel be $r$. According to the problem, the radius of the larger wheel is $R = 2r$.

Since both wheels are fixed coaxially, they rotate together and undergo the same angular displacement $\theta$ in a given time interval.

The string supporting block A is wound around the smaller wheel. Therefore, the distance travelled by A is:
$x = r\theta$

The string supporting block B is wound around the larger wheel. Therefore, the distance travelled by B is:
$y = R\theta = (2r)\theta$

Substituting $r\theta = x$ into the equation for $y$, we get:
$y = 2x$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed cylindrical tube, which contains some water but is not completely filled, rests in a horizontal plane. When the tube is rotated about its perpendicular bisector, the moment of inertia of the water about this axis
options: increases if the rotation is clockwise and decreases if it is anticlockwise
questions.solution:
When the closed cylindrical tube is rotated about its perpendicular bisector, the water inside experiences a centrifugal force directed radially outwards from the axis of rotation.

Due to this centrifugal force, the water moves towards the ends of the tube, away from the axis of rotation.

The moment of inertia of a mass distribution is given by $I = \int r^2 dm$, where $r$ is the perpendicular distance of the mass element $dm$ from the axis of rotation.

Since the water is displaced towards the ends, the effective distance $r$ of the mass elements from the axis increases.

Therefore, the moment of inertia of the water about this axis increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the moment of inertia for a uniform semicircular wire of mass $M$ and radius $r$, taken about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane.
questions.solution:
Consider an elemental mass $dm$ on the semicircular wire.

The distance of this elemental mass from the axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane is equal to the radius $r$ of the semicircle.

The moment of inertia of this elemental mass about the given axis is $dI = (dm)r^2$.

Integrating over the entire semicircular wire, the total moment of inertia is $I = \int dI = \int r^2 dm$.

Since the distance $r$ is constant for all points on the wire, it can be taken out of the integral:

$I = r^2 \int dm$

The total mass of the semicircular wire is given as $\int dm = M$.

Therefore, $I = Mr^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a body whose centre of mass is situated at the origin, with three of its particles located at $(a, 0, 0)$, $(0, a, 0)$, and $(0, 0, a)$. The moments of inertia of this body about the $X$ and $Y$ axes are each $0.20 \text{ kg-m}^2$. The moment of inertia about the $Z$-axis
options: cannot be deduced using the given information
questions.solution:
The moments of inertia of a body about the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes are given by:

$I_x = \sum m_i (y_i^2 + z_i^2)$

$I_y = \sum m_i (x_i^2 + z_i^2)$

$I_z = \sum m_i (x_i^2 + y_i^2)$

Adding the moments of inertia about the $X$ and $Y$ axes, we get:

$I_x + I_y = \sum m_i (x_i^2 + y_i^2 + 2z_i^2)$

$I_x + I_y = \sum m_i (x_i^2 + y_i^2) + 2 \sum m_i z_i^2$

$I_x + I_y = I_z + 2 \sum m_i z_i^2$

The perpendicular axis theorem, which states $I_z = I_x + I_y$, is only applicable for a two-dimensional planar body (lamina) lying entirely in the $XY$ plane, where $z_i = 0$ for all particles.

The problem specifies that one of the particles is located at $(0, 0, a)$, which implies $z_i \neq 0$. Thus, the body is three-dimensional and the perpendicular axis theorem cannot be applied.

Since the complete mass distribution of the body is not provided, the value of $\sum m_i z_i^2$ is unknown. Therefore, the moment of inertia about the $Z$-axis, $I_z$, cannot be determined from the given information.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body rotates nonuniformly about a fixed vertical axis within an inertial frame. The resultant force acting on a particle of the body that is not located on the axis is
options: horizontal and skew with the axis
questions.solution:
Let the fixed vertical axis of rotation be the z-axis. The angular velocity of the body is $\vec{\omega} = \omega \hat{k}$ and its angular acceleration is $\vec{\alpha} = \alpha \hat{k}$, where $\alpha eq 0$ since the rotation is non-uniform.

Let the position vector of a particle of mass $m$ be $\vec{r} = r \hat{e}_r + z \hat{k}$, where $r > 0$ because the particle is not located on the axis.

The acceleration of the particle has two components:
Centripetal acceleration: $\vec{a}_c = -\omega^2 r \hat{e}_r$
Tangential acceleration: $\vec{a}_t = \alpha r \hat{e}_ heta$

The resultant force acting on the particle is given by Newton's second law:
$\vec{F} = m\vec{a} = -m\omega^2 r \hat{e}_r + m\alpha r \hat{e}_ heta$

Since the resultant force $\vec{F}$ has no component in the $\hat{k}$ (vertical) direction, it lies entirely in a horizontal plane.

The torque of this force about the z-axis is:
$ au_z = r F_ heta = m\alpha r^2$

Since $\alpha eq 0$ and $r eq 0$, the torque $ au_z$ is non-zero. If the line of action of the force intersected the z-axis, its torque about the axis would be zero. Because the torque is non-zero, the force does not intersect the axis of rotation.

A horizontal line that does not intersect the vertical axis is skew with the axis. Therefore, the resultant force is horizontal and skew with the axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cubical block of mass $M$ and edge length $a$ slides down a rough inclined plane of inclination $	heta$ at a uniform velocity. Determine the magnitude of the torque produced by the normal force on the block about its centre.
questions.solution:
Since the cubical block moves down the inclined plane with uniform velocity, its translational and rotational accelerations are zero.

For translational equilibrium along the inclined plane, the frictional force $f$ balances the component of weight:

$f = Mg\sin\theta$

For rotational equilibrium about the centre of the block, the net torque must be zero. The forces acting on the block are weight $Mg$, normal force $N$, and friction $f$.

The weight $Mg$ acts through the centre, so its torque about the centre is zero.

The frictional force $f$ acts along the bottom surface, which is at a perpendicular distance of $\dfrac{a}{2}$ from the centre. The torque due to friction about the centre is:

$\tau_{f} = f \times \dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}Mga\sin\theta$

To maintain rotational equilibrium, the torque produced by the normal force $N$ about the centre must balance the torque produced by friction. Therefore, the magnitude of the torque due to the normal force is equal to the magnitude of the torque due to friction:

$\tau_{N} = \tau_{f} = \dfrac{1}{2}Mga\sin\theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin circular ring having mass $M$ and radius $r$ rotates about its axis with an angular speed $\omega$. Subsequently, two particles, each of mass $m$, are attached at diametrically opposite points. The resulting angular speed of the ring will be
questions.solution:
Initial moment of inertia of the ring is $I_1 = M r^2$.

Initial angular speed is $\omega_1 = \omega$.

When two particles each of mass $m$ are attached at diametrically opposite points on the ring, the new moment of inertia becomes $I_2 = M r^2 + 2m r^2 = (M + 2m) r^2$.

Since no external torque acts on the system, the angular momentum is conserved.

$I_1 \omega_1 = I_2 \omega_2$

$M r^2 \omega = (M + 2m) r^2 \omega_2$

$\omega_2 = \dfrac{M \omega}{M + 2m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person sitting securely on a rotating stool has his arms extended. If he folds his arms, what happens to his angular momentum about the axis of rotation?
questions.solution:
Since no external torque acts on the system about the axis of rotation, the angular momentum of the system must be conserved.

According to the law of conservation of angular momentum, $L = I\omega = \text{constant}$.

When the person folds his arms, his moment of inertia $I$ decreases. To conserve angular momentum, his angular velocity $\omega$ increases, but the total angular momentum $L$ remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose two bodies possess an identical geometrical shape, with the first being made of aluminium and the second of iron. If their moments of inertia are denoted by $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively, then
options: the relationship between $I_1$ and $I_2$ is dependent on the actual shapes of the bodies
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of a body is given by $I = \int r^2 dm = \int r^2 \rho dV$, where $\rho$ is the density of the material and $dV$ is the volume element.

Since both bodies have identical geometrical shapes, the integral $\int r^2 dV$ is the same for both.

Therefore, the moment of inertia is directly proportional to the density of the material, $I \propto \rho$.

The density of aluminium is less than the density of iron, $\rho_{Al} < \rho_{Fe}$.

Hence, the moment of inertia of the aluminium body is less than that of the iron body, which gives $I_1 < I_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular velocity of a scooter's engine (and consequently its wheel) is proportional to the petrol input per second. The scooter is travelling along a frictionless road with a uniform velocity. When the petrol input is increased by $10\%$, the linear velocity of the scooter increases by
questions.solution:
The scooter is travelling on a frictionless road. The forward force required to change the linear velocity of a vehicle is provided by the frictional force between the tires and the road.

Since the road is frictionless, no external horizontal force acts on the scooter. When the petrol input is increased, the angular velocity of the wheels increases, causing them to slip or spin faster on the road without providing any forward thrust.

By Newton's first law of motion, the linear velocity of the scooter remains uniform.

Thus, the increase in linear velocity is $0\%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere, a hollow sphere, and a disc, all possessing identical mass and radius, are released from the top of a smooth incline. The least time to reach the bottom will be taken by
options: all will take the same time
questions.solution:
Since the incline is smooth, there is no frictional force to provide the necessary torque for rolling.

Therefore, all the objects will purely slide down the incline without rolling.

The acceleration of any object sliding down a smooth incline of angle $\theta$ is given by $a = g \sin \theta$, which is independent of the mass, radius, and shape (moment of inertia) of the body.

Since all objects start from rest, cover the same distance $s$, and have the same acceleration, the time taken $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g \sin \theta}}$ will be the same for all of them.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere, a hollow sphere, and a disc, all sharing identical mass and radius, are positioned at the top of an incline and released. The friction coefficients between the objects and the incline are identical and are insufficient to permit pure rolling. The minimum time to reach the bottom will be taken by
options: all will require the same time
questions.solution:
Since the friction is insufficient to permit pure rolling, all the objects will slip as they move down the incline.

The kinetic friction acting on each object is $f_k = \mu mg \cos \theta$.

The equation of motion for the center of mass is given by $mg \sin \theta - f_k = ma$.

Substituting the value of $f_k$, we get $a = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$.

The linear acceleration $a$ is independent of the shape, mass, radius, and moment of inertia of the objects.

Since all objects are released from rest and travel the same distance along the incline with the same acceleration, they will all require the same time to reach the bottom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere, a hollow sphere and a disc, all having the same mass and radius, are released from rest at the top of an incline. The friction coefficients are identical and not sufficient to allow pure rolling. The smallest kinetic energy at the bottom of the incline will be achieved by
options: all will achieve same kinetic energy
questions.solution:
Since the friction is not sufficient to allow pure rolling, all the objects will slip as they move down the incline. The kinetic friction force acting on each object is $f_k = \mu M g \cos\theta$.

The linear acceleration of the center of mass for all objects is given by:
$a = g \sin\theta - \mu g \cos\theta$

Since $a$ is independent of the shape and moment of inertia, all three objects will take the same time $t$ to reach the bottom and will have the same translational velocity $v = at$. Thus, their translational kinetic energies $K_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^2$ will be identical.

The angular acceleration of each object is:
$\alpha = \dfrac{\tau}{I} = \dfrac{f_k R}{I}$

The angular velocity at the bottom of the incline is:
$\omega = \alpha t = \dfrac{f_k R t}{I}$

The rotational kinetic energy at the bottom is:
$K_{\text{rot}} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \left( \dfrac{f_k R t}{I} \right)^2 = \dfrac{(f_k R t)^2}{2 I}$

The total kinetic energy is:
$K = K_{\text{trans}} + K_{\text{rot}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^2 + \dfrac{(f_k R t)^2}{2 I}$

Since $M$, $v$, $f_k$, $R$, and $t$ are the same for all three objects, the total kinetic energy $K$ is inversely related to the moment of inertia $I$. To find the smallest kinetic energy, we need the object with the largest moment of inertia.

The moments of inertia for the given objects are:
Solid sphere: $I = \dfrac{2}{5} M R^2 = 0.4 M R^2$
Disc: $I = \dfrac{1}{2} M R^2 = 0.5 M R^2$
Hollow sphere: $I = \dfrac{2}{3} M R^2 \approx 0.67 M R^2$

The hollow sphere has the largest moment of inertia, which means it will acquire the smallest rotational kinetic energy. Therefore, the hollow sphere will achieve the smallest total kinetic energy at the bottom of the incline.

Answer: the hollow sphere

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A string of negligible thickness is wound multiple times around a cylinder resting on a rough horizontal surface. A man positioned at a distance $l$ from the cylinder grasps one end of the string and pulls the cylinder toward himself, as shown in the figure. Assuming no slipping occurs anywhere, the length of the string that passes through the man's hands by the time the cylinder reaches him is
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the center of mass of the cylinder be $v$ and its angular velocity be $\omega$.

For pure rolling on a stationary horizontal surface, the condition is $v = \omega R$, where $R$ is the radius of the cylinder.

The string is pulled from the topmost point of the cylinder. The velocity of this point is the vector sum of the translational velocity and the tangential velocity due to rotation:

$v_{\text{top}} = v + \omega R = v + v = 2v$

The cylinder reaches the man when its center of mass has travelled a distance $l$. The time taken is $t$, so $\int_{0}^{t} v \, dt = l$.

The length of the string that passes through the man's hands is the total distance moved by the end of the string (the topmost point of the cylinder) in the same time $t$.

Distance $= \int_{0}^{t} v_{\text{top}} \, dt = \int_{0}^{t} 2v \, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{t} v \, dt = 2l$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel rolls along a plane surface such that its centre moves at a speed $v_0$. What is the speed of a particle situated on the rim of the wheel at the same level as the centre?
questions.solution:
For a wheel in pure rolling, the velocity of its centre is $v_0$ and its angular velocity is $\omega = \dfrac{v_0}{R}$, where $R$ is the radius of the wheel.

The velocity of any point on the wheel is the vector sum of the velocity of the centre and the velocity of the point with respect to the centre.

Let the wheel move in the horizontal direction with velocity $\vec{v}_{c} = v_0 \hat{i}$.

For a particle on the rim at the same level as the centre, its position vector relative to the centre is horizontal. Hence, its velocity relative to the centre is vertical, given by $\vec{v}_{pc} = \pm \omega R \hat{j} = \pm v_0 \hat{j}$.

The velocity of the particle with respect to the ground is $\vec{v}_{p} = \vec{v}_{c} + \vec{v}_{pc} = v_0 \hat{i} \pm v_0 \hat{j}$.

The speed of the particle is the magnitude of its velocity:

$|\vec{v}_{p}| = \sqrt{v_0^2 + (\pm v_0)^2} = \sqrt{2}v_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel of radius $20 \text{ cm}$ is pushed to move on a rough horizontal surface. It travels a distance of $60 \text{ cm}$ on the road during the time it completes one revolution about its centre. Assuming the linear and angular accelerations are uniform, the frictional force exerted by the surface on the wheel is
options: along the velocity of the wheel
perpendicular to the velocity of the wheel
opposite to the velocity of the wheel
questions.solution:
Distance travelled by the wheel in one revolution, $s = 60 \text{ cm}$

Angle rotated by the wheel in one revolution, $\theta = 2\pi \text{ rad}$

Distance the wheel would travel if it were in pure rolling, $s_{rolling} = R\theta = 20 \times 2\pi = 40\pi \approx 125.6 \text{ cm}$

Since $s < s_{rolling}$, the average translational velocity is less than the average rotational velocity ($v < R\omega$).

The velocity of the lowest point of the wheel (point of contact) with respect to the ground is given by $v_{contact} = v - R\omega$.

Since $v < R\omega$, we get $v_{contact} < 0$. This implies that the point of contact is slipping in the backward direction, opposite to the translational motion of the wheel.

Kinetic friction always opposes the relative motion of the surfaces in contact. Since the point of contact slips backwards, the frictional force exerted by the surface on the wheel must act in the forward direction.

Therefore, the frictional force acts along the velocity of the wheel.
